rows.name: SPX drawdown from 52w high
rows.tip: How far the S&P 500 has fallen from its 52-week high. 0% means at a new high. Deeper negatives signal a selloff in progress.

rows.name: Forward P/E drawdown (12m)
rows.tip: How far the forward 12-month P/E ratio has dropped from its trailing peak. Compression here means either prices fell or earnings estimates rose, both worth investigating.

rows.name: Forward P/E level (12m)
rows.tip: The S&P 500 price divided by the next 12 months of estimated earnings. Higher means stocks are more expensive relative to expected profits. Compare to the 5y average to gauge whether the market is rich or cheap.

rows.name: n12m forward EPS growth
rows.tip: Year-over-year change in the street's forward 12-month EPS estimate. Rising means analysts are upgrading earnings expectations. Falling means downgrades are outpacing upgrades.

rows.name: S&P 500 net profit margin
rows.tip: Net profit margin for the S&P 500 aggregate from FactSet. Expanding margins support earnings growth even if revenue slows. Contracting margins mean costs are growing faster than sales.

rows.name: Implied equity risk premium
rows.tip: Damodaran-style 5y DCF on S&P 500 dividends + buybacks, grown at FactSet consensus fading to risk-free rate. Higher = cheaper market, lower = complacency.

rows.name: RSP / SPY ratio
rows.tip: Equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) divided by cap-weight S&P 500 (SPY). Rising ratio means breadth is improving, more stocks participating in the rally. Falling ratio means a handful of mega-caps are carrying the index.

rows.name: Sector fwd P/E dispersion
rows.tip: Highest sector forward P/E minus lowest sector forward P/E (from FactSet). Wide dispersion = big valuation gaps across sectors, rotation/stock-picking opportunity. Narrow dispersion = broad-based valuation regime where sector calls matter less.

rows.name: EPS revision (4w, %)
rows.tip: 4-week rolling mean of weekly change in S&P 500 forward 12-month EPS (FactSet). Positive = analysts net-upgrading earnings. Negative = net-downgrading. Strong earnings-revision proxy for sentiment and macro momentum.

rows.name: CNN fear/greed
rows.tip: CNN's composite of 7 market indicators scored 0 to 100. Below 25 is extreme fear (historically a contrarian buy signal). Above 75 is extreme greed (historically a warning to trim risk).

rows.name: CFTC: S&P 500 spec net
rows.tip: Net non-commercial positioning in E-mini S&P 500 futures (CFTC Legacy report). Positive = speculators net long equities. Negative = net short. Extreme readings are historically contrarian signals; neutral most of the time.

rows.name: CFTC: 10y UST spec net
rows.tip: Net non-commercial positioning in 10-year UST futures (CFTC Legacy report). Positive = speculators net long bonds (pricing lower yields ahead). Crowded shorts have historically preceded rate-cut pivots and bond rallies.

rows.name: CFTC: Gold spec net
rows.tip: Net non-commercial positioning in gold futures (CFTC Legacy report). Tracks speculative gold demand separate from physical/ETF flows. Extremes historically coincide with local price tops (over-long) and bottoms (over-short).

rows.name: CFTC: WTI spec net
rows.tip: Net non-commercial positioning in WTI crude futures (CFTC Legacy report). High net longs signal positioning risk if supply or demand shifts. Deep net shorts are rare and often occur near cyclical price lows.

rows.name: IWM / SPY ratio
rows.tip: Russell 2000 small-caps (IWM) divided by S&P 500 (SPY). Rising ratio means small-caps are outperforming, a sign of broad risk appetite. Falling ratio means investors are hiding in large-cap quality.

rows.name: SPHB / SPLV (risk appetite)
rows.tip: S&P 500 High Beta (SPHB) divided by S&P 500 Low Volatility (SPLV). Rising = risk appetite improving, investors favoring volatile names. Falling = investors rotating into defensive low-vol stocks. Cleaner breadth-proxy than size ratios.

rows.name: XLY / XLP (cyclical / defensive)
rows.tip: Consumer Discretionary (XLY) divided by Consumer Staples (XLP). Classic cyclical/defensive rotation gauge. Rising = economic optimism, discretionary spending expected to grow. Falling = consumers pulling back to staples, defensive rotation underway.

rows.name: Europe (VGK)
rows.tip: Vanguard FTSE Europe ETF. Broad European equity exposure.

rows.name: Emerging (EEM)
rows.tip: iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF. Broad EM exposure including China, India, Brazil.

rows.name: China (FXI)
rows.tip: iShares China Large-Cap ETF. Tracks the 50 largest Chinese companies.

rows.name: Japan (EWJ)
rows.tip: iShares MSCI Japan ETF. Broad Japanese equity exposure.

rows.name: WTI crude ($/bbl)
rows.tip: West Texas Intermediate crude oil spot price in dollars per barrel. Rising oil can signal strong demand or supply disruption. Feeds into inflation expectations and energy sector earnings.

rows.name: USD broad index
rows.tip: Trade-weighted US dollar index against a broad basket of currencies. A strong dollar hurts US exporters and emerging markets. A weak dollar boosts commodity prices and multinational earnings.

rows.name: Gold ($/oz futures)
rows.tip: Gold futures front-month contract in USD per troy ounce. Traditional safe haven and inflation hedge. Rising gold alongside rising equities can signal inflation anxiety.

rows.name: Copper ($/lb futures)
rows.tip: Copper futures front-month contract in USD per pound. Dr. Copper: sensitive to global industrial demand, especially China manufacturing and construction. Rising copper typically signals cyclical economic strength.

rows.name: Copper / Gold (cyclical)
rows.tip: Copper divided by gold. A classic risk-on / risk-off cyclical indicator. Rising = growth expectations improving (copper outperforming safe-haven gold). Falling = growth concerns building (gold outperforming industrial metals).

rows.name: Gold / Silver (stress)
rows.tip: Gold price divided by silver price. A stress gauge for precious metals. Rising ratio = safe-haven demand for gold over industrial-use silver (stress). Falling ratio = cyclical risk appetite returning to silver.

rows.name: VIX (equity vol)
rows.tip: CBOE Volatility Index, the market's 30-day implied volatility for S&P 500 options. Below 15 is complacent calm. 15 to 20 is normal. Above 25 means real fear is being priced into options.

rows.name: MOVE (bond vol)
rows.tip: ICE BofA MOVE Index, implied volatility of US Treasury options. The bond market's version of VIX. Spikes here often precede or confirm equity volatility events.

rows.name: VIX / VIX3M ratio
rows.tip: VIX divided by VIX3M (3-month implied vol). Below 1 = contango (normal, calm). Above 1 = backwardation (fear, hedging demand). Sustained readings above 1 signal structural risk aversion.

rows.name: SKEW (tail risk)
rows.tip: CBOE SKEW Index measures the price of tail-risk hedging via out-of-money puts. Higher values mean options traders are paying more for crash protection. Readings above 150 historically precede sharp drawdowns.

rows.name: Put/Call ratio
rows.tip: CBOE total put/call ratio. Puts divided by calls across all options. Above 1.0 = more puts than calls (bearish hedging, often contrarian buy). Below 0.7 = complacency (often contrarian sell). Extremes signal panic or greed.

rows.name: Fed Funds rate
rows.tip: Effective Federal Funds rate — the actual overnight policy rate. Differs slightly from the FOMC target range midpoint. Drives short-term borrowing costs across the entire economy.

rows.name: 3m T-Bill
rows.tip: 3-month Treasury bill yield, the closest proxy for the Fed's policy rate. Moves with Fed funds rate expectations. A drop signals the market is pricing in rate cuts.

rows.name: 2y nominal
rows.tip: 2-year Treasury yield, the most rate-sensitive maturity. Moves on Fed expectations more than the 10y. When 2y > 10y the curve is inverted, historically a recession signal.

rows.name: 10y nominal
rows.tip: 10-year US Treasury nominal yield. The benchmark rate that prices mortgages, corporate bonds, and equity valuations. Rising yields compress P/E multiples; falling yields support them.

rows.name: 30y nominal
rows.tip: 30-year Treasury yield, the long end of the curve. Drives mortgage rates, pension liabilities, and long-duration asset valuations. Reflects long-term inflation and real-growth expectations.

rows.name: 10y real (TIPS)
rows.tip: 10-year Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities yield (the real yield). Nominal 10y minus this equals breakeven inflation. Rising real yields tighten financial conditions even if nominal rates are flat.

rows.name: 5y breakeven
rows.tip: 5-year breakeven inflation rate (nominal 5y minus real 5y). The market's best guess at average inflation over the next 5 years. Rising breakevens mean the bond market expects more inflation.

rows.name: 10y breakeven
rows.tip: 10-year breakeven inflation (nominal 10y minus real 10y). The market's best guess at average inflation over the next 10 years. Broader / more stable measure than the 5y breakeven.

rows.name: 10y minus 2y curve
rows.tip: 10-year minus 2-year Treasury spread, the classic recession indicator. Negative (inverted) has preceded every US recession since 1970. Re-steepening after inversion often signals the recession is imminent.

rows.name: 10y minus 3m curve
rows.tip: 10-year Treasury yield minus 3-month T-bill yield. Positive means a normal upward-sloping curve. Negative (inverted) has preceded every US recession since 1970.

rows.name: 2y minus Fed Funds (cut expectations)
rows.tip: 2-year Treasury yield minus Fed Funds rate. A clean proxy for market-implied rate expectations. Negative = market pricing Fed cuts over the next 2 years. Positive = market pricing hikes. Magnitude roughly scales with expected cut count.

rows.name: IG OAS (bps)
rows.tip: Investment-grade corporate bond option-adjusted spread over Treasuries. Measures the extra yield investors demand for corporate credit risk. Widening spreads signal stress; tight spreads signal confidence.

rows.name: HY OAS (bps)
rows.tip: High-yield (junk) bond spread over Treasuries. The most sensitive real-time measure of credit stress. Spikes here often lead equity selloffs by days or weeks.

rows.name: Chicago Fed NFCI
rows.tip: Chicago Fed National Financial Conditions Index. Negative values mean loose conditions (easy money, tight spreads). Positive values mean tight conditions (stress in funding markets).

rows.name: Net liquidity YoY (WALCL − RRP)
rows.tip: Year-over-year change in Fed net liquidity = Fed balance sheet (WALCL) minus reverse repo (RRP). Tracks the actual dollars flowing into or out of the financial system. Expanding net liquidity has historically coincided with equity-friendly conditions.

rows.name: Initial jobless claims (4w avg)
rows.tip: 4-week moving average of initial unemployment insurance claims. The most timely read on labor market health. Sustained moves above 300K have historically signaled recession.

rows.name: SPX vs LT Govt corr (13w)
rows.tip: 13-week rolling correlation between S&P 500 and long-term Treasury weekly returns. Negative correlation is normal (stocks down, bonds up as a hedge). Positive correlation means stocks and bonds are selling off together, a signal of inflation-driven regime.

rows.name: Gold vs LT Govt corr (13w)
rows.tip: 13-week rolling correlation between gold and long-term Treasury weekly returns. Positive correlation means both are acting as safe havens together. Negative correlation means one is being favored over the other as a hedge.

economy.name: CPI YoY
economy.tip: Year-over-year change in the Consumer Price Index (all items). The headline inflation rate on every front page after release day. Fed targets 2%. Above 3% keeps rate cuts off the table.

economy.name: Core CPI YoY
economy.tip: Year-over-year CPI excluding food and energy. The inflation measure the Fed actually watches for policy decisions. Stickier than headline because it strips out volatile components.

economy.name: Core PCE YoY
economy.tip: Year-over-year Core PCE (ex food + energy) — the Fed's ACTUAL inflation target. Different from CPI: broader basket weights that shift with spending patterns, typically runs 30-50bp below Core CPI. If Powell talks inflation, this is what he means.

economy.name: PPI YoY
economy.tip: Year-over-year change in the Producer Price Index (final demand). Measures input cost inflation for producers before it hits consumers. Rising PPI with flat CPI means margin compression is coming. Falling PPI is deflationary signal.

economy.name: Non-farm payrolls
economy.tip: Month-over-month change in total US nonfarm payrolls (thousands of jobs). The single most market-moving data release. Published first Friday of each month. Above +200K is strong; below +100K signals a cooling labor market.

economy.name: Sahm Rule
economy.tip: Sahm Rule: 3-month avg unemployment rate minus 12-month low. When it exceeds 0.50, recession is historically already underway. Developed by former Fed economist Claudia Sahm — cleaner than the yield curve.

economy.name: Consumer sentiment
economy.tip: University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Monthly survey of household confidence. Leads consumer spending which is 70% of US GDP. Below 60 is historically recessionary territory. Above 80 is strong.

economy.name: Retail sales MoM
economy.tip: Month-over-month percent change in advance retail sales. Real-time signal of whether consumers are actually spending. Confirms or contradicts what sentiment surveys suggest.

economy.name: Industrial prod. YoY
economy.tip: Year-over-year change in the Industrial Production Index. Measures actual factory, mine, and utility output. Best available proxy for ISM PMI on FRED. Negative readings signal manufacturing contraction.

economy.name: Housing starts YoY
economy.tip: Year-over-year change in new residential construction starts. Most rate-sensitive sector — shows whether higher rates are biting the real economy.

economy.name: ISM Services PMI
economy.tip: ISM Services PMI, a diffusion index of US services-sector business conditions. Above 50 signals expansion, below 50 contraction. Services is ~70% of US GDP, so this is often the single most important monthly read on the economy.

economy.name: Empire State Mfg
economy.tip: Empire State Manufacturing Survey general-activity index from the NY Fed. Positive values mean conditions improved that month; negative means contraction. Released mid-month as an early read on manufacturing before the national surveys.

economy.name: Philly Fed Mfg
economy.tip: Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Survey current-activity index. Same diffusion-index methodology as Empire State, covering the Third Fed District. Correlates strongly with ISM Manufacturing PMI and moves markets on release day.

economy.name: M2 YoY
economy.tip: Year-over-year change in the M2 money supply (cash, checking, savings, retail money funds). Leads inflation and risk-asset returns by roughly 12 to 18 months historically. Negative readings are rare and historically precede tight-liquidity regimes.

economy.name: Shiller CAPE
economy.tip: Cyclically-Adjusted P/E (PE10). Real S&P 500 price divided by 10-year average real earnings. Smooths cyclical earnings volatility that makes forward P/E noisy. The gold-standard long-term valuation metric. >30 historically precedes poor 10-year forward returns.

economy.name: Buffett Indicator
economy.tip: Total US market cap (Wilshire 5000) divided by nominal GDP. Warren Buffett called this 'the best single measure of where valuations stand'. The absolute level is less meaningful than where it sits in its 5y range — elevated = overvalued, depressed = undervalued.
